name: Protocol
canonical_definition: A document that describes the objective(s), design, methodology, statistical considerations, and organization of a trial.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.44

name: CSR (Clinical Study Report)
canonical_definition: A written description of a trial/study of any therapeutic, prophylactic, or diagnostic agent conducted in human subjects, in which the clinical and statistical description, presentations, and analyses are fully integrated into a single report.
definition_source: ICH E3 Section 1

name: CSR Synopsis
canonical_definition: A brief summary (synopsis) of the clinical study report that provides a concise description of the trial.
definition_source: ICH E3 Appendix — Synopsis Template

name: IB (Investigator's Brochure)
canonical_definition: A compilation of the clinical and nonclinical data on the investigational product(s) which is relevant to the study of the investigational product(s) in human subjects.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.36

name: SAP (Statistical Analysis Plan)
canonical_definition: A document that contains a more technical and detailed elaboration of the principal features of the analysis described in the protocol, and includes detailed procedures for executing the statistical analysis of the primary and secondary variables and other data.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 7

name: ICF (Informed Consent Form)
canonical_definition: A document by which a subject voluntarily confirms his or her willingness to participate in a particular trial, after having been informed of all aspects of the trial that are relevant to the subject's decision to participate.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.28

name: DSUR (Development Safety Update Report)
canonical_definition: A common standard for periodic reporting on drugs under development (including marketed drugs that are the subject of further study) among the ICH regions, to provide a periodic assessment of safety information during the clinical development period of a drug.
definition_source: ICH E2F Section 1.1

name: PBRER (Periodic Benefit-Risk Evaluation Report)
canonical_definition: A report intended to be a common standard for periodic benefit-risk evaluation reporting on marketed products (including products approved under exceptional circumstances/conditional approval) among the ICH regions.
definition_source: ICH E2C(R2) Section 1

name: CTD Summary
canonical_definition: Module 2 of the Common Technical Document (CTD), which provides summaries of the quality (2.3), nonclinical (2.4, 2.6) and clinical (2.5, 2.7) information contained in Modules 3, 4 and 5 respectively.
definition_source: ICH M4 — Organisation of the CTD

name: FDA Guidance Document
canonical_definition: Documents prepared for FDA staff, applicants/sponsors, and the public that describe the Agency's interpretation of or policy on a regulatory issue. Guidance documents do not establish legally enforceable responsibilities.

name: FDA Review Document
canonical_definition: The multi-discipline review document prepared by FDA reviewers during evaluation of a marketing application, including medical, statistical, clinical pharmacology, and pharmacology/toxicology reviews.
definition_source: FDA MAPP 6010.3 Rev. 4

name: eCTD Module
canonical_definition: The electronic Common Technical Document (eCTD) is an interface for industry to agency transfer of regulatory information. It is organized into five modules: Module 1 (Regional), Module 2 (CTD Summaries), Module 3 (Quality), Module 4 (Nonclinical), Module 5 (Clinical).
definition_source: ICH M8 — eCTD Specification v4.0

name: CRF (Case Report Form)
canonical_definition: A printed, optical, or electronic document designed to record all of the protocol required information to be reported to the sponsor on each trial subject.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.11

name: Annotated CRF (aCRF)
canonical_definition: A blank CRF with annotations showing the relationship of CRF data collection fields to the corresponding SDTM domains and variables, required as part of the eCTD submission.
definition_source: FDA Study Data Technical Conformance Guide v4.5, Section 4.1.3

name: Data Dictionary
canonical_definition: A centralized repository of information about data such as meaning, relationships to other data, origin, usage, and format. In clinical trials, typically includes variable names, labels, data types, controlled terminology, and validation rules.
definition_source: CDISC Glossary v14.0

name: FDA-Approved Labeling
canonical_definition: The FDA-approved label is the official description of a drug product which includes indication; who should take it; adverse events; instructions for uses in pregnancy, children, and other populations; and safety information for the patient.
definition_source: 21 CFR 201.56 and 201.57 (Physician Labeling Rule)

name: NDA 505(b)(1) (New Drug Application — Full)
canonical_definition: An application submitted under section 505(b)(1) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act for any drug for which the investigations described in clause (A) of subsection (b)(1) and relied upon by the applicant for approval of the application were conducted by or for the applicant.
definition_source: 21 CFR 314.50; FD&C Act Section 505(b)(1)

name: NDA 505(b)(2)
canonical_definition: An application submitted under section 505(b)(2) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act that contains full reports of investigations of safety and effectiveness but where at least some of the information required for approval comes from studies not conducted by or for the applicant and for which the applicant has not obtained a right of reference or use.
definition_source: 21 CFR 314.54; FD&C Act Section 505(b)(2)

name: BLA (Biologics License Application)
canonical_definition: A request for permission to introduce, or deliver for introduction, a biologic product into interstate commerce (21 CFR 601.2). A biologics license shall be issued to the applicant if the applicable standards established in the act and regulations are met.

name: ANDA (Abbreviated New Drug Application)
canonical_definition: An application submitted under section 505(j) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act for a duplicate of a drug product approved under section 505(c) of the Act and relies on FDA's finding that the previously approved drug product is safe and effective.
definition_source: 21 CFR 314.94; FD&C Act Section 505(j)

name: IND (Investigational New Drug Application)
canonical_definition: An exemption from the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act that allows an investigational drug to be shipped in interstate commerce before a marketing application for the drug has been approved. The IND provides the legal basis for testing an investigational new drug in humans.

name: 510(k) Premarket Notification
canonical_definition: A premarket submission made to FDA to demonstrate that the device to be marketed is at least as safe and effective, that is, substantially equivalent, to a legally marketed device that is not subject to premarket approval.
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 510(k); 21 CFR 807 Subpart E
note: Device pathway — lower priority for CRO drug-focused MVP

name: PMA (Premarket Approval)
canonical_definition: The FDA process of scientific and regulatory review to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of Class III medical devices. Class III devices are those that support or sustain human life, are of substantial importance in preventing impairment of human health, or which present a potential, unreasonable risk of illness or injury.
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 515; 21 CFR 814
note: Device pathway — lower priority for CRO drug-focused MVP

name: De Novo Classification
canonical_definition: A regulatory pathway for novel medical devices for which there is no legally marketed predicate device, and which are determined to be low-to-moderate risk. A De Novo request provides a pathway to classify these novel devices into Class I or Class II.
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 513(f)(2); 21 CFR 860.260
note: Device pathway — lower priority for CRO drug-focused MVP

name: Fast Track Designation
canonical_definition: FDA shall facilitate the development, and expedite the review of, a drug designated as a fast track product. A drug shall be considered a fast track product if it is intended for the treatment of a serious or life-threatening disease or condition and demonstrates the potential to address unmet medical needs.
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 506(b); 21 CFR 312.300

name: Breakthrough Therapy Designation
canonical_definition: A drug that is intended to treat a serious condition and preliminary clinical evidence indicates that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over available therapy on a clinically significant endpoint(s).
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 506(a); FDASIA Section 902

name: Accelerated Approval
canonical_definition: FDA may grant accelerated approval to a drug for a serious or life-threatening disease or condition upon a determination that the drug has an effect on a surrogate endpoint that is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit, or on a clinical endpoint that can be measured earlier than irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM).
definition_source: 21 CFR 314.500; FD&C Act Section 506(c)

validity_periods.note: Subpart H regulations; amended by FDORA 2022

name: Priority Review
canonical_definition: Priority Review designation means FDA's goal is to take action on an application within 6 months (compared to 10 months under standard review). A Priority Review designation will direct overall attention and resources to the evaluation of applications for drugs that, if approved, would be significant improvements in the safety or effectiveness of the treatment, diagnosis, or prevention of serious conditions.
definition_source: PDUFA V Performance Goals; FDA Guidance 'Priority Review' (2014)

name: RMAT (Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy)
canonical_definition: A regenerative medicine therapy, including cell therapy, therapeutic tissue engineering products, human cell and tissue products, and combination products using such therapies or products, that is intended to treat, modify, reverse, or cure a serious or life-threatening disease or condition and for which preliminary clinical evidence indicates that the drug has the potential to address unmet medical needs.
definition_source: FD&C Act Section 506(g); 21st Century Cures Act Section 3033

name: Orphan Drug Designation
canonical_definition: A designation granted to a drug or biologic intended for the safe and effective treatment, diagnosis, or prevention of rare diseases/disorders that affect fewer than 200,000 people in the US, or that affect more than 200,000 persons but for which there is no reasonable expectation of recovering development costs.
definition_source: Orphan Drug Act (1983); 21 CFR 316

name: FDA Meeting — Type A
canonical_definition: Type A meetings are those that are necessary for an otherwise stalled drug development program to proceed (e.g., dispute resolution meetings, special protocol assessment meetings following receipt of a complete response letter). FDA will schedule Type A meetings to occur within 30 days of FDA receipt of the meeting request.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Formal Meetings Between FDA and Sponsors or Applicants of PDUFA Products' (2017), Section III.A

name: FDA Meeting — Type B
canonical_definition: Type B meetings include pre-IND, end-of-Phase 1, end-of-Phase 2, and pre-NDA/BLA meetings. FDA will schedule Type B meetings to occur within 60 days of receipt of the meeting request.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Formal Meetings Between FDA and Sponsors or Applicants of PDUFA Products' (2017), Section III.B

name: FDA Meeting — Type C
canonical_definition: Type C meetings are any meetings other than Type A and Type B meetings. FDA will schedule Type C meetings to occur within 75 days of receipt of the meeting request.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Formal Meetings Between FDA and Sponsors or Applicants of PDUFA Products' (2017), Section III.C

name: Complete Response Letter (CRL)
canonical_definition: A written communication to an applicant from FDA usually describing all of the deficiencies that the agency has identified in a new drug application or abbreviated new drug application that must be satisfactorily addressed before it can be approved.

name: Refuse to File (RTF)
canonical_definition: An action taken by FDA within 60 days of receipt of an NDA or BLA when the application is not sufficiently complete to permit a substantive review. The filing review evaluates whether the application is sufficiently complete for the agency to conduct a substantive review.
definition_source: 21 CFR 314.101(d); FDA Guidance 'Refuse to File' (2017)

name: PDUFA Date
canonical_definition: The Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) date is the target date by which FDA intends to complete its review of a marketing application. For standard reviews, the goal is 10 months from the date of filing; for priority reviews, the goal is 6 months.
definition_source: PDUFA VII (2022-2027) Performance Goals

name: Advisory Committee (AdCom)
canonical_definition: FDA advisory committees provide independent expert advice to the agency on scientific, technical, and policy matters. Advisory committees for drugs and biologics are established under the Federal Advisory Committee Act (FACA) and provide recommendations on approval decisions.
definition_source: 21 CFR Part 14; Federal Advisory Committee Act (FACA)

name: IND-Enabling Phase
canonical_definition: The preclinical development stage encompassing the nonclinical studies required to support the initial clinical trial in humans, including pharmacology, toxicology, and CMC activities necessary for IND submission.
definition_source: D2V internal

name: Phase I
canonical_definition: Clinical trials that include the initial introduction of an investigational new drug into humans. Phase 1 studies are typically closely monitored and may be conducted in patients or normal volunteer subjects. These studies are designed to determine the metabolism and pharmacologic actions of the drug in humans, the side effects associated with increasing doses, and, if possible, to gain early evidence on effectiveness.

name: Phase I/II
canonical_definition: A combined Phase I/Phase II trial that evaluates both the safety/tolerability and preliminary efficacy of an investigational product, often used in oncology to identify the recommended Phase 2 dose while gathering initial efficacy signals.
definition_source: D2V internal

name: Phase II
canonical_definition: Phase 2 includes the controlled clinical studies conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of the drug for a particular indication or indications in patients with the disease or condition under study and to determine the common short-term side effects and risks associated with the drug.

name: Phase II/III
canonical_definition: A combined Phase II/Phase III trial, often using adaptive design, that begins as a dose-finding or proof-of-concept study and seamlessly transitions to a confirmatory pivotal study based on interim analysis results.
definition_source: D2V internal

name: Phase III
canonical_definition: Phase 3 studies are expanded controlled and uncontrolled trials. They are performed after preliminary evidence suggesting effectiveness of the drug has been obtained, and are intended to gather the additional information about effectiveness and safety that is needed to evaluate the overall benefit-risk relationship of the drug and to provide an adequate basis for physician labeling.

name: Phase IV
canonical_definition: Post-marketing studies to delineate additional information including the drug's risks, benefits, and optimal use. These studies could include, but would not be limited to, studying different doses or schedules of administration, use in other patient populations or other stages of the disease, or use over a longer period of time.

name: NDA/BLA Submission Phase
canonical_definition: The regulatory submission stage where the sponsor compiles and submits the complete marketing application (NDA or BLA) to FDA for review, including all required eCTD modules.
definition_source: D2V internal

name: Small Molecule
canonical_definition: A low molecular weight organic compound (typically under 900 daltons) that may regulate a biological process, often serving as the active pharmaceutical ingredient in conventional drug products regulated under the NDA pathway.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'ANDAs for Certain Highly Purified Synthetic Peptide Drug Products' (2021); general pharmacology definition

name: Monoclonal Antibody
canonical_definition: A type of protein made in the laboratory that can bind to substances in the body, including cancer cells. There are many kinds of monoclonal antibodies. A monoclonal antibody is made so that it binds to only one substance. Monoclonal antibodies are regulated as biological products under the BLA pathway.
definition_source: NCI Dictionary of Cancer Terms; 42 USC 262 (PHS Act for BLA pathway)

name: Cell Therapy
canonical_definition: The administration of whole, living cells to a patient for the treatment of a disease. Human cells used in cell therapy are regulated as biological products under section 351 of the Public Health Service Act.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Regulatory Considerations for Human Cells, Tissues, and Cellular and Tissue-Based Products' (2017); 21 CFR 1271

name: Gene Therapy
canonical_definition: Human gene therapy seeks to modify or manipulate the expression of a gene or to alter the biological properties of living cells for therapeutic use. FDA considers human gene therapy products to include all products that mediate their effects by transcription and/or translation of transferred genetic material.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Long Term Follow-Up After Administration of a Gene Therapy Product' (2020), Section I

name: RNA Therapeutic
canonical_definition: Therapeutic products based on ribonucleic acid (RNA) including mRNA vaccines, small interfering RNA (siRNA), antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs), and other RNA-based modalities that modulate gene expression.
definition_source: D2V internal

name: Combination Product
canonical_definition: A product comprised of any combination of a drug and a device; a biological product and a device; a drug and a biological product; or a drug, device, and a biological product.

name: OS (Overall Survival)
canonical_definition: The time from randomization (or registration) to death from any cause. For subjects who have not died, OS is censored at the date of the last known vital status assessment. OS is considered the most reliable cancer endpoint.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.A

name: PFS (Progression-Free Survival)
canonical_definition: The time from randomization to objective tumor progression or death from any cause, whichever occurs first. PFS can reflect tumor growth and therefore can be evaluated before a survival benefit is demonstrated.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.C

name: ORR (Overall Response Rate)
canonical_definition: The proportion of patients with tumor size reduction of a predefined amount and for a minimum time period. ORR is the sum of partial responses plus complete responses. ORR is a direct measure of drug antitumor activity.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.B

name: DFS (Disease-Free Survival)
canonical_definition: The time from randomization to recurrence of tumor or death from any cause, used in the adjuvant setting after definitive surgery or radiation therapy.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.D

name: EFS (Event-Free Survival)
canonical_definition: The time from randomization to any event including disease progression, treatment discontinuation for any reason, or death, whichever occurs first. Used in hematologic malignancies and neoadjuvant settings.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.E (implied); commonly used per STEEP criteria

name: pCR (Pathological Complete Response)
canonical_definition: The absence of invasive cancer in the breast and axillary nodes at the time of surgery following neoadjuvant therapy (ypT0/Tis ypN0). Used as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval in early-stage breast cancer.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Pathological Complete Response in Neoadjuvant Treatment of High-Risk Early-Stage Breast Cancer' (2020)

name: MRD (Minimal Residual Disease)
canonical_definition: The detection of residual cancer cells below the threshold of conventional morphologic assessment, using techniques such as flow cytometry or next-generation sequencing. Under evaluation as a potential surrogate endpoint in hematologic malignancies.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Hematologic Malignancies: Regulatory Considerations for Use of Minimal Residual Disease in Development of Drug and Biological Products' (2020)

name: DOR (Duration of Response)
canonical_definition: The time from documentation of tumor response to disease progression. DOR supplements ORR by characterizing the durability of the antitumor response.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018), Section IV.B.3

name: PRO (Patient-Reported Outcome)
canonical_definition: Any report of the status of a patient's health condition that comes directly from the patient, without interpretation of the patient's response by a clinician or anyone else.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Patient-Reported Outcome Measures: Use in Medical Product Development to Support Labeling Claims' (2009), Section II.A

name: Biomarker
canonical_definition: A defined characteristic that is measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or responses to an exposure or intervention, including therapeutic interventions.
definition_source: FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group, BEST Resource (2016)

name: Companion Diagnostic
canonical_definition: An in vitro diagnostic device that provides information that is essential for the safe and effective use of a corresponding therapeutic product. The use of an IVD companion diagnostic device with a therapeutic product is stipulated in the instructions for use in the labeling of both the diagnostic device and the corresponding therapeutic product.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'In Vitro Companion Diagnostic Devices' (2014), Section III

name: RECIST (Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors)
canonical_definition: A set of published rules that define when tumors in cancer patients improve (respond), stay the same (stabilize), or worsen (progress) during treatment. RECIST 1.1 defines measurable disease, methods of measurement, and criteria for evaluation of response.
definition_source: Eisenhauer EA, et al. Eur J Cancer 2009;45:228-247 (RECIST 1.1); referenced in FDA Oncology endpoint guidance

name: AE (Adverse Event)
canonical_definition: Any untoward medical occurrence in a patient or clinical investigation subject administered a pharmaceutical product and which does not necessarily have a causal relationship with this treatment.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.2

name: SAE (Serious Adverse Event)
canonical_definition: Any untoward medical occurrence that at any dose: results in death, is life-threatening, requires inpatient hospitalization or prolongation of existing hospitalization, results in persistent or significant disability/incapacity, or is a congenital anomaly/birth defect.
definition_source: ICH E6(R2) Section 1.50; 21 CFR 312.32

name: Cmax (Maximum Plasma Concentration)
canonical_definition: The maximum (or peak) serum concentration that a drug achieves in a specified compartment or test area of the body after the drug has been administered and before the administration of a second dose.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Bioanalytical Method Validation' (2018); CDISC SDTM PK domain

name: AUC (Area Under the Curve)
canonical_definition: The integral of the concentration-time curve after a single dose or in steady state. AUC is a measure of total systemic exposure to the drug.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Bioanalytical Method Validation' (2018); CDISC SDTM PK domain

name: Estimand
canonical_definition: A precise description of the treatment effect reflecting the clinical question posed by the trial objective. It summarises at a population-level what the outcomes would be in the same patients under the different treatment conditions being compared. An estimand is described by four attributes: population, variable (endpoint), specification of how intercurrent events are reflected in the variable, and population-level summary.
definition_source: ICH E9(R1) Addendum Section A.2

name: Intercurrent Event Strategy
canonical_definition: Events occurring after treatment initiation that either preclude observation of the variable or affect its interpretation. ICH E9(R1) defines five strategies: treatment policy, composite, hypothetical, while on treatment, and principal stratum.
definition_source: ICH E9(R1) Addendum Section A.3

name: Multiplicity Adjustment
canonical_definition: Statistical methods to control the overall probability of making at least one false positive conclusion when multiple hypotheses are tested simultaneously. Adjustment is required when multiple primary endpoints, multiple treatment comparisons, or interim analyses are planned.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 2.2.5; FDA Guidance 'Multiple Endpoints in Clinical Trials' (2022)

validity_periods.note: ICH E9; updated by FDA 2022 guidance

name: NI Margin (Non-Inferiority Margin)
canonical_definition: The non-inferiority margin (M) specifies the largest difference between the experimental treatment and the active control that would be clinically acceptable. M must be less than the entire effect of the active control compared to placebo (M1), and the margin used in testing (M2) may be smaller than M1.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Non-Inferiority Clinical Trials to Establish Effectiveness' (2016), Section III

name: Alpha Spending Function
canonical_definition: A function that specifies how the overall type I error probability is allocated across interim analyses in a group sequential design. Common forms include the O'Brien-Fleming and Pocock boundaries, and the Lan-DeMets alpha spending approach.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 4.5 (Interim Analysis); Lan & DeMets, Biometrika 1983

name: Gatekeeping Procedure
canonical_definition: A class of multiple testing procedures that use a hierarchical or graphical structure to control the familywise error rate across families of hypotheses, allowing alpha to be recycled from rejected hypotheses to downstream hypotheses.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Multiple Endpoints in Clinical Trials' (2022), Section V; Dmitrienko et al. Stat Med 2003

name: Interim Analysis
canonical_definition: Any analysis intended to compare treatment arms with respect to efficacy or safety at any time prior to the formal completion of a trial. Interim analyses require pre-specification and appropriate alpha-spending to protect the type I error rate.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 4.5

name: Sample Size Calculation
canonical_definition: The determination of the number of subjects needed to demonstrate a clinically meaningful treatment effect with adequate statistical power while controlling the type I error rate, based on assumptions about the endpoint distribution, expected effect size, and variability.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 3.5

name: Statistical Power
canonical_definition: The probability that the trial will detect a treatment difference, if a true treatment difference of a specified amount exists. A power of 0.80 or 0.90 is typically used in the design of confirmatory clinical trials.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 3.5

name: Type I Error Control
canonical_definition: The probability of falsely rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true (false positive). In confirmatory trials, the type I error rate is conventionally set at 0.05 (two-sided) or 0.025 (one-sided). Control of this error rate across multiple testing situations is a regulatory requirement.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 2.2.5

name: Sensitivity Analysis
canonical_definition: A series of analyses conducted with the intent of exploring the robustness of the results of the primary analysis. Sensitivity analyses should examine the impact of deviations from the assumptions underlying the primary analysis, including the handling of protocol deviations, missing data, and outliers.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 5.2.4; ICH E9(R1) Section A.4

name: Missing Data Handling
canonical_definition: Statistical approaches to address incomplete data in clinical trials, including multiple imputation, mixed models for repeated measures (MMRM), and pattern mixture models. The choice of method should be pre-specified and aligned with the estimand framework.
definition_source: NRC Report 'The Prevention and Treatment of Missing Data in Clinical Trials' (2010); ICH E9(R1)

name: ITT Population (Intent-to-Treat)
canonical_definition: The principle that asserts that the effect of a treatment policy can be best assessed by evaluating on the basis of the intention to treat a subject (i.e., the planned treatment regimen) rather than the actual treatment given. It has the consequence that subjects allocated to a treatment group should be followed up, assessed and analysed as members of that group irrespective of their compliance with the planned course of treatment.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 5.2.1

name: mITT Population (Modified Intent-to-Treat)
canonical_definition: A subset of the ITT population that typically excludes subjects who did not meet key entry criteria, never received study treatment, or had no post-baseline efficacy assessment. The definition must be pre-specified and justified.
definition_source: D2V internal; referenced in FDA statistical reviews but no single canonical definition

validity_periods.note: Common practice, no single regulatory definition

name: PP Population (Per-Protocol)
canonical_definition: The set of data generated by the subset of subjects who complied with the protocol sufficiently to ensure that these data would be likely to exhibit the effects of treatment, according to the underlying scientific model. Compliance covers such considerations as exposure to treatment, availability of measurements, and absence of any major protocol violations.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 5.2.2

name: Safety Population
canonical_definition: The analysis population used for safety analyses, typically defined as all subjects who received at least one dose of study treatment. Subjects are analyzed according to the treatment actually received.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 5.2.3; industry standard practice

name: Superiority Design
canonical_definition: A trial designed to detect a difference between treatments. The null hypothesis is that there is no difference between the treatments, and the alternative hypothesis is that a difference exists.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 2.2.2

name: Non-Inferiority Design
canonical_definition: A trial designed to show that the experimental treatment is not worse than the reference treatment by more than a pre-specified non-inferiority margin.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 2.2.3; FDA Guidance 'Non-Inferiority Clinical Trials' (2016)

name: Equivalence Design
canonical_definition: A trial designed to confirm the absence of a meaningful difference between treatments, using two-sided confidence intervals against symmetric equivalence margins.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 2.2.4

name: Single-Arm Design
canonical_definition: A trial design in which all enrolled subjects receive the investigational treatment, with no concurrent control arm. Efficacy is assessed by comparison to a historical control rate or an external benchmark.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics' (2018); used for accelerated approval based on ORR

name: Adaptive Design
canonical_definition: A clinical trial design that allows for prospectively planned modifications to one or more aspects of the design based on accumulating data from subjects in the trial. Modifications may include sample size re-estimation, dropping or adding treatment arms, or dose modification.
definition_source: FDA Guidance 'Adaptive Designs for Clinical Trials of Drugs and Biologics' (2019), Section II

name: CDASH Domain — Demographics (DM)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Demographics domain collects subject-level demographic information including age, sex, race, ethnicity, and country. Variables follow CDISC CDASH Implementation Guide standards.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Section 5 (Demographics Domain)

name: CDASH Domain — Vital Signs (VS)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Vital Signs domain collects measurements of vital sign parameters including blood pressure, heart rate, body temperature, and respiratory rate.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Vital Signs Domain

name: CDASH Domain — Adverse Events (AE)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Adverse Events domain collects information about adverse events including the event term, start/end dates, severity, seriousness criteria, causality assessment, and outcome.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Adverse Events Domain

name: CDASH Domain — Concomitant Medications (CM)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Concomitant/Prior Medications domain collects information about medications and therapies other than study treatment used by subjects during or prior to the trial.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Concomitant Medications Domain

name: CDASH Domain — Medical History (MH)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Medical History domain collects the subject's significant medical history and current conditions prior to the start of the trial.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Medical History Domain

name: CDASH Domain — Laboratory Tests (LB)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Laboratory domain collects laboratory test results including hematology, chemistry, urinalysis, and other clinical laboratory assessments.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Laboratory Domain

name: CDASH Domain — ECG (EG)
canonical_definition: The CDASH ECG Test Results domain collects electrocardiogram test results including interval measurements (QT, QTc, PR, QRS) and overall interpretation.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, ECG Domain

name: CDASH Domain — Physical Examination (PE)
canonical_definition: The CDASH Physical Examination domain collects findings from the physical examination of subjects, including body systems assessed and findings observed.
definition_source: CDISC CDASHIG v2.2, Physical Examination Domain

name: SDTM (Study Data Tabulation Model)
canonical_definition: A standard structure for the collection and submission of non-clinical and clinical data to regulatory authorities. SDTM provides a standard for organizing study data into datasets and defining standard variables, enabling the regulatory authorities to process data submissions efficiently.
definition_source: CDISC SDTM Implementation Guide v3.4; FDA Data Standards Catalog

validity_periods.note: CDISC SDTM v1.0; FDA mandate from 2017

name: Controlled Terminology
canonical_definition: Sets of standardized, controlled terms maintained by CDISC for use in clinical study data collection and submission. These codelists ensure consistent representation of data values across studies and organizations.
definition_source: CDISC Controlled Terminology (published by NCI EVS); FDA Data Standards Catalog

name: Visit Schedule
canonical_definition: The planned sequence of study visits and assessments defined in the protocol, including visit windows, required procedures at each visit, and timing relative to randomization or treatment start.
definition_source: D2V internal; concept derived from ICH E6(R2) protocol requirements and CDASHIG visit structure

name: Edit Check
canonical_definition: Programmatic data validation rules applied to CRF data to identify discrepancies, inconsistencies, or out-of-range values requiring query resolution. Edit checks are part of the data management plan.
definition_source: D2V internal; concept referenced in CDISC CDASH and industry DM standards

name: Query Management
canonical_definition: The process of identifying, issuing, tracking, and resolving data queries during clinical trial data management to ensure data quality and integrity.
definition_source: D2V internal; referenced in ICH E6(R2) Section 5.18.4 (monitoring and data management)

name: Database Lock
canonical_definition: The formal process of freezing the clinical trial database after all data have been entered, verified, and all queries resolved, such that no further changes can be made without a formal unlocking procedure. Database lock triggers the start of the final statistical analysis.
definition_source: D2V internal; referenced in ICH E6(R2) Section 5.5.3 (data handling)

name: Medical Coding (MedDRA / WHODrug)
canonical_definition: The standardized coding of clinical trial data using controlled dictionaries: MedDRA (Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities) for adverse events and medical history, and WHODrug (WHO Drug Dictionary) for concomitant medications.
definition_source: ICH MedDRA Management Committee; WHO Collaborating Centre for International Drug Monitoring

name: Subgroup Analysis
canonical_definition: Evaluation of treatment effects in subsets of the trial population defined by baseline characteristics such as demographics, disease severity, biomarker status, or geographic region. Pre-specified subgroup analyses should be clearly distinguished from post-hoc exploratory analyses.
definition_source: ICH E9 Section 5.7; FDA Guidance 'Evaluation of Sex-Specific Data in Medical Device Clinical Studies' (2014)

name: Therapeutic Area — Oncology
canonical_definition: The branch of medicine that deals with the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer. In clinical trials, oncology is the most active therapeutic area with specific regulatory guidance for endpoints, trial design, and biomarker-driven development.
definition_source: D2V internal; FDA CDER Oncology Center of Excellence scope

name: Therapeutic Area — CNS
canonical_definition: Central Nervous System therapeutic area encompassing neurological and psychiatric conditions including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, depression, and schizophrenia.
definition_source: D2V internal; FDA CDER Division of Neurology scope

name: Therapeutic Area — Cardiovascular
canonical_definition: Therapeutic area encompassing diseases of the heart and blood vessels including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and hypertension.
definition_source: D2V internal; FDA CDER Division of Cardiology and Nephrology scope

name: Therapeutic Area — Rare Disease
canonical_definition: Diseases or conditions that affect fewer than 200,000 persons in the United States. Rare diseases may qualify for orphan drug designation and associated regulatory incentives.
definition_source: Orphan Drug Act (1983); Rare Diseases Act (2002)

name: Therapeutic Area — Immunology
canonical_definition: Therapeutic area encompassing immune-mediated diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease, lupus, and multiple sclerosis.
definition_source: D2V internal; FDA CDER Division of Rheumatology and Transplant Medicine scope

name: Therapeutic Area — Infectious Disease
canonical_definition: Therapeutic area encompassing diseases caused by pathogenic microorganisms including bacteria, viruses, parasites, and fungi. Includes antimicrobial, antiviral, and vaccine development.
definition_source: D2V internal; FDA CDER Division of Anti-Infective Products scope

name: Evidence Tier 1 — Binding
canonical_definition: Laws, statutes, and codified regulations that have the force of law. Compliance is mandatory and non-negotiable. Examples: 21 CFR Parts 11/50/56/312/314, FD&C Act, PHS Act.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Evidence Tier Framework

name: Evidence Tier 2 — Authoritative
canonical_definition: Final FDA guidance documents, ICH guidelines at Step 4, and consensus standards adopted by reference. While not legally binding, they represent the Agency's current thinking and deviation requires strong justification.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Evidence Tier Framework; 21 CFR 10.115

name: Evidence Tier 3 — Informative
canonical_definition: Draft FDA guidance, peer-reviewed publications, textbooks, and educational materials. Useful for context and decision support but subject to change and not directly enforceable.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Evidence Tier Framework

name: Evidence Tier 4 — Experiential
canonical_definition: CRO internal SOPs, expert opinion, historical project experience, and lessons learned. Valuable for operational guidance but must never override higher-tier evidence.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Evidence Tier Framework

name: Knowledge Type — Execution Rule
canonical_definition: A mandatory operational rule derived from regulatory requirements or validated best practices that MUST be injected into an Agent's system prompt before task execution. Execution rules are the highest priority knowledge type in the KB Engine.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Knowledge Architecture

name: Knowledge Type — Reference
canonical_definition: Reference knowledge including regulatory documents, guidelines, clinical study data, and literature that provides context and evidence for decision-making but is not directly injected as operational rules.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Knowledge Architecture

name: Knowledge Type — Template
canonical_definition: Reusable document templates and structured formats for clinical documents (protocols, CSRs, SAPs, CRFs) that encode best practices and regulatory requirements into standardized structures.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Knowledge Architecture

name: Verification Level L0 (Dual-Extraction Verified)
canonical_definition: The highest verification level in the KB Engine trust framework, achieved when a numerical value or factual assertion has been independently extracted from two different sources or by two independent extraction methods, with results matching within acceptable precision.
definition_source: D2V internal — KB Engine Trust Framework
